sidebar.title: Classification History
sidebar.name: Classification History
sidebar.des: Aristotle (Ancient Greece): The Beginning. Aristotle was the earliest to attempt a more scientific basis for classification. He used simple morphological characters to classify plants into trees, shrubs and herbs. He also divided animals into two groups, those which had red blood and those that did not.

sidebar.title: PHYLUM 1
sidebar.name: Porifera
sidebar.des: Commonly known as sponges. These are primitive, multicellular, marine animals with a porous body that filters water to obtain food and oxygen.

sidebar.title: PHYLUM 2
sidebar.name: Coelenterata
sidebar.des: Also known as Cnidaria (like Jellyfish and Corals). They are aquatic, mostly marine, with tentacles equipped with stinging cells called cnidoblasts.

sidebar.title: PHYLUM 3
sidebar.name: Ctenophora
sidebar.des: Commonly known as sea walnuts or comb jellies. They are exclusively marine and move using eight external rows of ciliated comb plates.

sidebar.title: PHYLUM 4
sidebar.name: Platyhelminthes
sidebar.des: The flatworms. They have a dorso-ventrally flattened body and are mostly endoparasites found in animals, including humans.

sidebar.title: PHYLUM 5
sidebar.name: Aschelminthes
sidebar.des: The roundworms (Nematoda). Their body is circular in cross-section. They can be free-living, aquatic, terrestrial, or parasitic.

sidebar.title: PHYLUM 6
sidebar.name: Annelida
sidebar.des: Segmented worms like earthworms and leeches. Their body surface is distinctly marked out into segments or metameres.

sidebar.title: PHYLUM 7
sidebar.name: Arthropoda
sidebar.des: The largest phylum, including insects, spiders, and crustaceans. They have jointed appendages and a hard chitinous exoskeleton.

sidebar.title: PHYLUM 8
sidebar.name: Mollusca
sidebar.des: The second largest animal phylum (snails, octopuses). They are soft-bodied animals usually covered by a calcareous shell.

sidebar.title: PHYLUM 9
sidebar.name: Echinodermata
sidebar.des: Spiny-bodied animals like starfish and sea urchins. They have an endoskeleton of calcareous ossicles and a unique water vascular system.

sidebar.title: PHYLUM 10
sidebar.name: Hemichordata
sidebar.des: A small group of worm-like marine animals, such as acorn worms. They have a cylindrical body composed of an anterior proboscis, a collar, and a trunk.

sidebar.title: PHYLUM 11
sidebar.name: Chordata
sidebar.des: Animals with a notochord, a dorsal hollow nerve cord, and paired pharyngeal gill slits. This includes all vertebrates (fish, amphibians, reptiles, birds, mammals).

classificationHistory.name: Aristotle (Ancient Greece): The Beginning
classificationHistory.description:
The first major classifier was the philosopher Aristotle. He grouped animals based on how they looked and moved.
• He divided animals into two main groups: animals with blood (vertebrates) and animals without blood (invertebrates).
• He further subdivided them according to their mode of transportation, such as land, water, and air.
This system was used for centuries. However, it grouped bats with birds because both fly, and whales with fish because both swim. Today, we know these are not accurate biological relationships.

classificationHistory.name: The Age of Exploration: The Expansion
classificationHistory.description:
As Europeans traveled the world, they discovered thousands of new plants and animals.
The simple blood versus no blood system could not handle the growing volume of discoveries. Scientists needed a better way to organize and document biodiversity.
This period created the demand for a more structured and universal classification system.

classificationHistory.name: Linnaeus: The Revolution
classificationHistory.description:
Carl Linnaeus, a Swedish botanist, revolutionized biological classification. Although he believed species were fixed, he created the organizational system we still use today.
• Binomial Nomenclature: Every species was given a two-part Latin name (Genus and species), such as Homo sapiens.
• Hierarchical System: He introduced ranked categories including Kingdom, Class, Order, Genus, and Species.
Linnaeus grouped organisms based on overall similarity, not evolutionary history, but his structured system became the foundation of modern taxonomy.

classificationHistory.name: Darwin to Today: Shift to Evolution
classificationHistory.description:
Everything changed after Charles Darwin proposed the theory of evolution by natural selection.
Scientists realized that similarities among organisms reflect shared ancestry.
• The focus shifted from simple organization to tracing evolutionary relationships (Phylogenetics).

classificationHistory.name: Modern Cladistics: DNA-Based Classification
classificationHistory.description:
Modern cladistics represents the most advanced stage in biological classification.
Unlike earlier systems that grouped organisms based mainly on physical similarity, cladistics classifies organisms according to their evolutionary relationships and shared ancestry.
• It focuses on common derived characteristics (synapomorphies) that indicate shared evolutionary history.
• Organisms are arranged into clades, which include an ancestor and all of its descendants.
• Modern classification heavily relies on molecular data, especially DNA sequencing, to determine how closely species are related.
This approach has reshaped taxonomy. For example, birds are now recognized as a group within reptiles, and classification is based on evolutionary branching patterns rather than superficial traits like wings or fins.

children: Members of Porifera, commonly known as sponges, are among the oldest living multicellular animals. They originated in the Precambrian era and have survived successfully up to the present day.

About 8,550 species of poriferans are known worldwide. Sponges reached their greatest diversity during the Cretaceous period. They play an important ecological role by contributing to reef formation and are a major source of biogenic silica, which forms flint deposits.
Sponges are ubiquitous, occurring in oceans across all latitudes of the world.
The scientific study of sponges is known as Parazoology.
Mostly asymmetrical; some show radial symmetry
No excretory organs; wastes removed by diffusion
Internal skeletal framework made of spicules or spongin
Adult sponges are sessile or sedentary organisms and remain attached to an underwater object called the substratum.
The body wall contains numerous small inhalant openings called ostia.
These ostia open into a single, central, and spacious cavity called the spongocoel or paragastric cavity.
Water leaves the body through one or a few large exhalant openings called the osculum.
This is the outer cellular layer, made up of:
Oval porocytes, which form the ostia
This is the inner cellular layer, composed of choanocytes (collar cells).
Choanocytes are the characteristic cells of Porifera and are responsible for:
This is a non-cellular, jelly-like matrix present between the pinacoderm and choanoderm.
It contains spongin fibres and numerous spicules
It also has many types of amoebocytes, derived from both pinacoderm and choanoderm
The body wall is supported by a skeleton made of:
The canal system, also called the water canal system, is a characteristic feature of all Porifera.
Water enters the sponge body through ostia, passes into the spongocoel, and leaves through the osculum.
The amount of water flowing through a sponge is very large and may reach up to 20,000 times its body volume per day.
Sponges reproduce by both asexual and sexual methods, which helps them survive and spread in different environmental conditions.
Asexual reproduction occurs by fragmentation and budding, which may be external or internal.
Internal buds, produced during unfavourable conditions, are called gemmules.
Sponges show a high power of regeneration, where even small body fragments can grow into a complete sponge.
All sponges are hermaphrodites, meaning the sexes are not separate.
Therefore, they are described as monoecious or bisexual.
Sexual reproduction takes place by the formation of haploid (n) male and female gametes.
Sperms are released into the water from one sponge and are carried by water currents into another sponge.
Fertilisation occurs inside the body of the sponge, where the sperm fertilises the ovum in situ.
Development includes intermediate free-swimming larval stages, such as amphiblastula and parenchymula, which help in the dispersal of the species.
Based on the nature of the skeleton, phylum Porifera is divided into three main classes.
Calcarea, commonly known as calcareous sponges, are characterized by a skeleton made of calcium carbonate spicules.
The spicules are composed of calcium carbonate.
They are commonly found in shallow coastal waters, though some species occur at greater depths.
About 400 species of calcareous sponges have been described.
Class Calcarea is divided into three subclasses based on the structure of their skeleton and spicules.
Hexactinellida, commonly known as glass sponges, are known for their delicate and intricate skeletons made of silica (glass-like material).
The skeleton consists of six-rayed siliceous spicules.
They are mainly found in deep-sea environments, usually at depths of 200 metres or more.
Approximately 600–700 species (around 680 documented species) are known.
The class Hexactinellida is divided into two subclasses based on skeletal and spicule structure.
Demospongiae, commonly known as demosponges, form the largest class of sponges.
The skeleton is made of spongin fibres, siliceous spicules (not six-rayed), or both.
This class includes more than 90% of all sponge species.
Demosponges are found in a wide range of habitats:
The class is divided into several subclasses, based on skeletal structure, canal systems, and other morphological features.

of coelenterates are known worldwide. They are the first animals to show true tissue-level organization and are commonly called stinging animals. Most coelenterates are marine, though a few occur in freshwater habitats. They may be free-swimming or sessile, and can occur as solitary or colonial forms. Some corals provide habitat for a variety of marine organisms. The structure of the coral skeleton is similar to that of human bone; therefore, it is used as a bone graft substitute.
The scientific study of coelenterates is known as
Incomplete digestive system; digestion is first extracellular and then intracellular
Through body surface by simple diffusion
Gastrovascular cavity helps in distribution of food
Metabolic wastes are expelled by simple diffusion through the body surface
Endoskeleton absent; corals possess calcareous exoskeleton
Diffuse nerve net formed by nerve cells

Digestive juices released inside the cavity act on the ingested food, and enzymes help in its breakdown.

Polyp stage is reduced and temporary.
Anthozoa include corals and sea anemones, in which only the polyp form is present.
Corals possess a hard calcareous skeleton composed of calcium carbonate (CaCO₃).

It is a colonial hydrozoan in which different zooids perform different functions.
A gas gland present inside the pneumatophore secretes gas, which helps the colony float on the water surface.
The body of Hydra is represented by the polyp form, which is elongated and cylindrical.
Locomotion in Hydra occurs by looping, somersaulting, gliding, walking, and floating.
A piece of Hydra can regenerate into a complete individual if it contains parts of the epidermis, gastrodermis, and interstitial cells.
The regenerated parts develop according to polarity; the region closer to the hypostome forms the oral end.
The mouth is situated at the tip of the hypostome, surrounded by 6–10 long, slender, contractile tentacles. This end is called the

During asexual reproduction (budding), bud-like outgrowths arise from the body and eventually separate as young Hydra.
During sexual reproduction, gonads form as small bulges on the body surface.

The Dorsiventrally Flattened Worms (Platys – flat; helmin – worm)
are the first group of animals to evolve a
body plan. Commonly known as flatworms, their bodies are leaf-like or ribbon-like, ideally suited for their role as either free-living scavengers or specialized endoparasites.
They represent a major evolutionary milestone: the development of
(head formation) and a dedicated excretory system. Whether it's the incredible regenerative powers of
or the complex life cycles of tapeworms, they are masters of adaptation.
, found in animals including humans, where they possess hooks and suckers for attachment.
. Specialized cells for osmoregulation and excretion.
First animals to have a 'head' with concentrated nervous tissue (ganglia) and eyespots.
Digestive system has a single opening; branched gastrovascular cavity distributes nutrients.
can regenerate an entire organism from a tiny fragment of its body.
(no body cavity). Space filled with Parenchyma tissue.
Thick tegument, hooks, and suckers for attachment. Direct absorption of nutrients.
The flatworms represent a major evolutionary milestone: the development of bilateral symmetry and triploblastic organization.
Platyhelminthes pioneered the bilateral triploblastic design, paving the way for the complex organ systems seen in higher animals.

. They may be free-living (aquatic or terrestrial) or parasitic in plants and animals.
They represent a landmark in evolution: the first animals to possess a
(a body cavity not fully lined by mesoderm) and a
Sexual dimorphism is often distinct—females are frequently longer than males.
Fluid-filled body cavity acting as a hydrostatic skeleton.
Features a mouth, a highly muscular pharynx, and an anus.
Removes body wastes from the body cavity through the excretory pore.
The masters of parasitic success, roundworms have evolved a tough cuticle and pseudocoelom to thrive in nearly every environment on Earth.

Aschelminthes represent a significant evolutionary step with their pseudocoelom and complete digestive system. They are highly adaptive, thriving as both free-living organisms and specialized parasites.
represent the pinnacle of worm-like evolution. They are distinguished by their
bodies, where the body is divided into many distinct rings or segments both externally and internally.
From the industrious earthworm that aerates our soils to the predatory marine sandworms (
) and specialized leeches, annelids exhibit a complex body plan featuring a true coelom and a sophisticated closed circulatory system.
Annelids were the first group to evolve
, where blood is pumped through a network of vessels.
Tubular structures that manage both osmoregulation and excretion.
Blood stays within vessels; features multiple 'hearts' (aortic arches).
A double ventral nerve cord with paired ganglia in every segment.
Closed type (except in some leeches). Hemoglobin is sometimes dissolved in plasma.
Longitudinal and circular muscles help in movement, aided by
Annelids represent a masterclass in modular engineering, featuring true segments and a closed circulatory system.
Annelids represent a masterclass in modular engineering. Their metameric design allows for specialized regional growth and complex movement strategies.
The Conquerors of Earth (Arthros – joint; podos – foot)
of Animalia, accounting for over two-thirds of all named species on Earth. They are the ultimate biological success story, having conquered the land, sea, and air.
Their defining characteristic is the possession of
that provides both protection and a framework for muscle attachment. From microscopic mites to giant spider crabs and soaring dragonflies, their diversity is unparalleled.
belong to this phylum, making it the most dominant group in biological history.
Chitinous cuticle that must be shed (moulting/ecdysis) to allow growth.
Blood (hemolymph) flows into large body spaces called sinus/hemocoel.
A network of tubes for direct gas exchange in insects.
Antennae, Compound eyes, Simple eyes, and
Mostly Dioecious. Fertilization is usually Internal. Oviparous development.
Arthropoda is the largest phylum of Animalia, accounting for over two-thirds of all named species on Earth.
. They are primarily aquatic (marine or freshwater), though many reside on land. They are defined by their unsegmented, soft bodies usually covered by a calcareous shell.

A typical mollusc features a distinct head, a muscular foot for locomotion, and a visceral hump containing the internal organs. The space between the hump and the mantle (the skin covering) holds the gills, used for both respiration and excretion.
Their unique rasping organ for feeding, the
, is a hallmark of this phylum.
A file-like rasping organ used for scraping or cutting food.
A soft layer of skin that covers the visceral hump and secretes the shell.
Feather-like gills located in the mantle cavity for breathing.
Tentacles and eyes are usually present on the head.
Head, Muscular Foot, and Visceral Hump.

(except in Cephalopods like Octopus/Squid which have
Molluscs represent a masters of survival, combining soft-bodied flexibility with extraordinary structural engineering in their shells.
Molluscs represent a masters of survival, combining soft-bodied flexibility with extraordinary structural engineering in their shells.
The Spiny-Skinned Wonders (Echinos – spiny; derma – skin)
are exclusively marine animals characterized by an endoskeleton of calcareous ossicles. They are arguably the most unique group in the animal kingdom, featuring a body plan based on
Their most distinctive feature is the
, a network of fluid-filled canals that powers their locomotion, food capture, and respiration. From the iconic starfish to the globular sea urchin, they are masters of the ocean floor.
—a key evolutionary link to higher animals.
A perforated plate that acts as an intake valve for the system.
Extend into each arm, carrying water to the tube feet.
Flexible, suction-cup-like structures used for walking and gripping prey.
Bulbs that contract to force water into the tube feet, causing them to extend.
No freshwater or terrestrial echinoderms exist.
Complete; mouth on ventral (lower) side and anus on dorsal (upper) side.
Sexes are separate; fertilization is external. High power of regeneration.
Echinoderms demonstrate the power of hydraulic engineering in biology, using sea water to power a complex sensory and locomotory system.
Echinoderms demonstrate the power of hydraulic engineering in biology, using sea water to power a complex and highly effective locomotory system.
The Acorn Worms (Hemi – half; chorda – cord)
was earlier considered a sub-phylum under Chordata but is now placed as a separate phylum under Non-chordates. They consist of a small group of worm-like,
They are the evolutionary "handshake" between invertebrates and vertebrates. They possess
(a chordate feature) but lack a true notochord, having instead a
—a rudimentary structure in the collar region.
is structurally similar to a notochord but is actually an outgrowth of the roof of the buccal cavity.
An anterior, club-shaped organ used for burrowing and food collection.
The short middle section where the mouth is located ventrally.
The long posterior part containing the gills, intestine, and gonads.
Strictly marine and mostly tubicolous (living in U-shaped burrows).
Simple, open type with a dorsal heart.
Dioecious; fertilization is external. Features a
Hemichordates are the ‘silent witnesses’ of our evolutionary past, bridging the structural divide between invertebrates and vertebrates.
Hemichordates are the "silent witnesses" of our evolutionary past, bridging the structural divide between the simpler invertebrates and the complex vertebrates.
is the most advanced animal phylum, characterized by the presence of a
at some stage in their lifecycle. This group ranges from the primitive lancelets to complex vertebrates like humans.

All chordates share four fundamental structural signatures: a dorsal hollow nerve cord, pharyngeal gill slits, a post-anal tail, and of course, the notochord. They represent the height of neurological and physiological complexity.
"All vertebrates are chordates, but all chordates are NOT vertebrates." (e.g., Protochordates).
A flexible, rod-like structure for support.
Located above the notochord; becomes the CNS.
Openings in the pharynx for respiration/feeding.
Extension of the body beyond the anus.
Terrestrial vertebrates with 4 limbs (Amphibia, Reptilia, Aves, Mammalia).
Chordates represent the ultimate expression of symmetry, cephalization, and neurological refinement in the animal kingdom.
Chordates represent the ultimate expression of symmetry, cephalization, and neurological refinement in the animal kingdom.

The first major classifier was the philosopher Aristotle. He grouped animals based on how they looked and moved.
• He divided animals into two main groups: animals with blood (vertebrates) and animals without blood (invertebrates).
• He further subdivided them according to their mode of transportation, such as land, water, and air.
This system was used for centuries. However, it grouped bats with birds because both fly, and whales with fish because both swim. Today, we know these are not accurate biological relationships.

As Europeans traveled the world, they discovered thousands of new plants and animals.
The simple blood versus no blood system could not handle the growing volume of discoveries. Scientists needed a better way to organize and document biodiversity.
This period created the demand for a more structured and universal classification system.
The Age of Exploration: The Expansion

props.alt: The Age of Exploration: The Expansion

children:
Carl Linnaeus, a Swedish botanist, revolutionized biological classification. Although he believed species were fixed, he created the organizational system we still use today.
• Binomial Nomenclature: Every species was given a two-part Latin name (Genus and species), such as Homo sapiens.
• Hierarchical System: He introduced ranked categories including Kingdom, Class, Order, Genus, and Species.
Linnaeus grouped organisms based on overall similarity, not evolutionary history, but his structured system became the foundation of modern taxonomy.

Everything changed after Charles Darwin proposed the theory of evolution by natural selection.
Scientists realized that similarities among organisms reflect shared ancestry.
• The focus shifted from simple organization to tracing evolutionary relationships (Phylogenetics).
Darwin to Today: Shift to Evolution

props.alt: Darwin to Today: Shift to Evolution

children:
Modern cladistics represents the most advanced stage in biological classification.
Unlike earlier systems that grouped organisms based mainly on physical similarity, cladistics classifies organisms according to their evolutionary relationships and shared ancestry.
• It focuses on common derived characteristics (synapomorphies) that indicate shared evolutionary history.
• Organisms are arranged into clades, which include an ancestor and all of its descendants.
• Modern classification heavily relies on molecular data, especially DNA sequencing, to determine how closely species are related.
This approach has reshaped taxonomy. For example, birds are now recognized as a group within reptiles, and classification is based on evolutionary branching patterns rather than superficial traits like wings or fins.
